From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American lifestyle and politics magazine

"New York Magazine" redirects here. For the eighteenth-century magazine, see [The New-York Magazine](/wiki/The_New-York_Magazine "The New-York Magazine"). For similarly named but unrelated magazines, see [The New Yorker](/wiki/The_New_Yorker "The New Yorker") and [The New York Times Magazine](/wiki/The_New_York_Times_Magazine "The New York Times Magazine").

*New York*

***New York*** is an American biweekly magazine concerned with life, culture, politics, and style generally, with a particular emphasis on [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City").

Founded by [Clay Felker](/wiki/Clay_Felker "Clay Felker") and [Milton Glaser](/wiki/Milton_Glaser "Milton Glaser") in 1968 as a competitor to *[The New Yorker](/wiki/The_New_Yorker "The New Yorker")* and *[The New York Times Magazine](/wiki/The_New_York_Times_Magazine "The New York Times Magazine")*, it was brasher in voice and more connected to contemporary city life and commerce, and became a cradle of [New Journalism](/wiki/New_Journalism "New Journalism").[[3]](#cite_note-nymag-3) Over time, it became more national in scope, publishing many noteworthy articles about American culture by writers such as [Tom Wolfe](/wiki/Tom_Wolfe "Tom Wolfe"), [Jimmy Breslin](/wiki/Jimmy_Breslin "Jimmy Breslin"), [Nora Ephron](/wiki/Nora_Ephron "Nora Ephron"), [Pete Hamill](/wiki/Pete_Hamill "Pete Hamill"), [Jacob Weisberg](/wiki/Jacob_Weisberg "Jacob Weisberg"), [Michael Wolff](/wiki/Michael_Wolff_(journalist) "Michael Wolff (journalist)"), [John Heilemann](/wiki/John_Heilemann "John Heilemann"), [Frank Rich](/wiki/Frank_Rich "Frank Rich"), and [Rebecca Traister](/wiki/Rebecca_Traister "Rebecca Traister"). It was among the first "[lifestyle magazines](/wiki/Lifestyle_magazine "Lifestyle magazine")" meant to appeal to both male and female audiences, and its format and style have been emulated by many American regional and city publications.

*New York* in its earliest days focused almost entirely on coverage of its namesake city, but beginning in the 1970s, it expanded into reporting and commentary on national politics, notably [Richard Reeves](/wiki/Richard_Reeves_(American_writer) "Richard Reeves (American writer)") on [Watergate](/wiki/Watergate_scandal "Watergate scandal"), [Joe Klein](/wiki/Joe_Klein "Joe Klein")'s early cover story about [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton"), [John Heilemann](/wiki/John_Heilemann "John Heilemann")'s reporting on the 2008 presidential election that led to his (and [Mark Halperin](/wiki/Mark_Halperin "Mark Halperin")'s) best-selling book *[Game Change](/wiki/Game_Change "Game Change")*, [Jonathan Chait](/wiki/Jonathan_Chait "Jonathan Chait")'s commentary, and [Olivia Nuzzi](/wiki/Olivia_Nuzzi "Olivia Nuzzi")'s reporting on the [first Trump administration](/wiki/First_presidency_of_Donald_Trump "First presidency of Donald Trump"). *[The New Republic](/wiki/The_New_Republic "The New Republic")* praised its "hugely impressive political coverage" during the presidency of [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama").[[4]](#cite_note-4) It is also known for its arts and culture criticism, its food writing (its restaurant critic [Adam Platt](/wiki/Adam_Platt "Adam Platt") won a [James Beard Award](/wiki/James_Beard_Award "James Beard Award") in 2009, and its Underground Gourmet critics Rob Patronite and Robin Raisfeld won two [National Magazine Awards](/wiki/National_Magazine_Awards "National Magazine Awards")), and its service journalism (its "Strategist" department won seven National Magazine Awards in eleven years).

Since its sale, redesign, and relaunch in 2004, the magazine has won several [National Magazine Awards](/wiki/National_Magazine_Awards "National Magazine Awards"), including the award for general excellence in 2006, 2007, 2010, 2011, 2014, and 2016, as well as the 2013 award for Magazine of the Year.[[5]](#cite_note-Haughney-5) Since the [Pulitzer Prize](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize "Pulitzer Prize") for [Criticism](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize_for_Criticism "Pulitzer Prize for Criticism") opened to magazines as well as newspapers in 2016, *New York*'s critics have won twice ([Jerry Saltz](/wiki/Jerry_Saltz "Jerry Saltz") in 2018, and [Andrea Long Chu](/wiki/Andrea_Long_Chu "Andrea Long Chu") in 2023) and been finalists three more times ([Justin Davidson](/wiki/Justin_Davidson "Justin Davidson") in 2020, Craig Jenkins in 2021, and [Sara Holdren](/wiki/Sara_Holdren "Sara Holdren") in 2024). In 2009, the *[Washington Post](/wiki/Washington_Post "Washington Post")* media critic [Howard Kurtz](/wiki/Howard_Kurtz "Howard Kurtz") wrote that "the nation's best and most-imitated city magazine is often not about the city—at least not in the overcrowded, traffic-clogged, [five-boroughs](/wiki/Boroughs_of_New_York_City "Boroughs of New York City") sense," observing that it was more regularly publishing political and cultural stories of national and international import.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

The magazine's first website, nymetro.com, was launched in 2001. In the early 21st century, the magazine began to diversify that online presence, introducing subject-specific websites under the nymag.com umbrella: [*Vulture*](/wiki/Vulture_(website) "Vulture (website)"), *[The Cut](/wiki/The_Cut_(New_York) "The Cut (New York)")*, *Intelligencer, The Strategist, [Curbed](/wiki/Curbed "Curbed")*, and *Grub Street*.[[7]](#cite_note-7) In 2018, New York Media, the parent company of *New York* magazine, launched a [digital subscription product](/wiki/Paywall "Paywall") for those sites.[[8]](#cite_note-8) On September 24, 2019, [Vox Media](/wiki/Vox_Media "Vox Media") announced that it had purchased *New York* magazine and its parent company, New York Media.[[9]](#cite_note-Tracy-9)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(magazine)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### 1960s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(magazine)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1960s")]

*New York* was created in 1963[[10]](#cite_note-10) as the Sunday-magazine supplement of the *[New York Herald Tribune](/wiki/New_York_Herald_Tribune "New York Herald Tribune")* newspaper. The *Herald Tribune*, then in financial difficulty, had recently been sold to [John Hay Whitney](/wiki/John_Hay_Whitney "John Hay Whitney"), and was looking to revitalize its business with an increased focus on editorial excellence, which included a relaunch of the Sunday edition and its magazine. Edited first by Sheldon Zalaznick and then by [Clay Felker](/wiki/Clay_Felker "Clay Felker"), the relaunched magazine, called *New York*, showcased the work of many talented *Tribune* contributors, including [Tom Wolfe](/wiki/Tom_Wolfe "Tom Wolfe"), [Barbara Goldsmith](/wiki/Barbara_Goldsmith "Barbara Goldsmith"), [Gail Sheehy](/wiki/Gail_Sheehy "Gail Sheehy"), [Dick Schaap](/wiki/Dick_Schaap "Dick Schaap"), and [Jimmy Breslin](/wiki/Jimmy_Breslin "Jimmy Breslin").[[11]](#cite_note-McLellan-11) The *Tribune* went out of business in 1966, and *New York* was briefly revived as part of a combined paper, the [*World Journal Tribune*](/wiki/New_York_World_Journal_Tribune "New York World Journal Tribune"), that lasted until May 1967. Shortly after the *WJT* closed, Felker and his partner, [Milton Glaser](/wiki/Milton_Glaser "Milton Glaser"), purchased the rights to the nameplate, backed by [Wall Street](/wiki/Wall_Street "Wall Street") bankers led by [Armand G. Erpf](/wiki/Armand_G._Erpf "Armand G. Erpf") (the magazine's first chairman, who Felker attributed as the financial architect of the magazine[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)) and C. Gerald Goldsmith (Barbara Goldsmith's husband at the time),[[3]](#cite_note-nymag-3)[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) and reincarnated the magazine as a stand-alone glossy weekly. Joining them was managing editor Jack Nessel, Felker's number-two at the *Herald Tribune.* *New York*'s first issue was dated April 8, 1968.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Several writers came from the magazine's earlier incarnation, including Breslin, Wolfe (who wrote "You and Your Big Mouth: How the Honks and Wonks Reveal the Phonetic Truth about Status" in the inaugural issue), and [George Goodman](/wiki/George_Goodman "George Goodman"), a financial writer who wrote under the pseudonym "[Adam Smith](/wiki/Adam_Smith "Adam Smith")." Glaser and his deputy Walter Bernard designed and laid out the magazine and hired many notable artists, including [Jim McMullan](/wiki/James_McMullan "James McMullan"), [Robert Grossman](/wiki/Robert_Grossman_(artist) "Robert Grossman (artist)"), and [David Levine](/wiki/David_Levine "David Levine"), to produce covers and illustrations.

Within a year, Felker had assembled a team of contributors who would come to define the magazine's voice. Breslin became a regular, as did [Nicholas Pileggi](/wiki/Nicholas_Pileggi "Nicholas Pileggi"), [Gail Sheehy](/wiki/Gail_Sheehy "Gail Sheehy"), and [Gloria Steinem](/wiki/Gloria_Steinem "Gloria Steinem"), who wrote a politics column. [Judith Crist](/wiki/Judith_Crist "Judith Crist") wrote movie reviews. [Harold Clurman](/wiki/Harold_Clurman "Harold Clurman") was hired as the theater critic, then replaced a few months later by [John Simon](/wiki/John_Simon_(critic) "John Simon (critic)"), who became notorious for his harsh reviews.[[17]](#cite_note-17) [Alan Rich](/wiki/Alan_Rich "Alan Rich") covered the classical-music scene. [Barbara Goldsmith](/wiki/Barbara_Goldsmith "Barbara Goldsmith") wrote a series called "The Creative Environment", in which she interviewed such subjects as [Marcel Breuer](/wiki/Marcel_Breuer "Marcel Breuer"), [I. M. Pei](/wiki/I._M._Pei "I. M. Pei"), [George Balanchine](/wiki/George_Balanchine "George Balanchine"), and [Pablo Picasso](/wiki/Pablo_Picasso "Pablo Picasso") about their process. [Gael Greene](/wiki/Gael_Greene "Gael Greene"), writing under the rubric "The Insatiable Critic", reviewed [restaurants](/wiki/Restaurant "Restaurant"), cultivating a [baroque](/wiki/Baroque "Baroque") writing style that leaned heavily on sexual [metaphor](/wiki/Metaphor "Metaphor").[[18]](#cite_note-18) The office for the magazine was on the top floor of the old Tammany Hall clubhouse at [207 East 32nd Street](/wiki/207_East_32nd_Street "207 East 32nd Street"), which Glaser owned.[[19]](#cite_note-19) The magazine did not consistently turn a profit in these early years: One board member, Alan Patricof, later said that "it may have touched into the black for a quarter, then out of it, but it was not significantly profitable."[[20]](#cite_note-20)

### 1970s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(magazine)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1970s")]

Wolfe, a regular contributor to the magazine, wrote a story in 1970 that captured the spirit of the magazine (if not the age): "[Radical Chic](/wiki/Radical_Chic "Radical Chic"): That Party at Lenny's". The controversial and often criticized[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) article described a benefit party for the [Black Panthers](/wiki/Black_Panthers "Black Panthers"), held in [Leonard Bernstein](/wiki/Leonard_Bernstein "Leonard Bernstein")'s apartment, in a collision of [high culture](/wiki/High_culture "High culture") and low that paralleled *New York* magazine's ethos and expressed Wolfe's interest in status and class.

In 1972, *New York*'s year-end issue incorporated a 30-page preview of the first issue of [*Ms.*](/wiki/Ms._magazine "Ms. magazine") magazine, edited by [Gloria Steinem](/wiki/Gloria_Steinem "Gloria Steinem").[[11]](#cite_note-McLellan-11) Gail Sheehy's "The Search for Grey Gardens", a cover story about the notorious [mother](/wiki/Edith_Ewing_Bouvier_Beale "Edith Ewing Bouvier Beale")-and-[daughter](/wiki/Edith_Bouvier_Beale "Edith Bouvier Beale") Beale household of East Hampton, led to the [Maysles brothers'](/wiki/Albert_and_David_Maysles "Albert and David Maysles") acclaimed [documentary](/wiki/Grey_Gardens "Grey Gardens").

As the 1970s progressed, Felker continued to broaden the magazine's editorial vision beyond Manhattan, covering [Richard Nixon](/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon") and the [Watergate](/wiki/Watergate "Watergate") scandal closely. He also launched [*New West*](/wiki/New_West_(magazine) "New West (magazine)"), a sister magazine on *New York*'s model that covered [California](/wiki/California "California") life, published in separate Northern California and Southern California editions. In 1976, [journalist](/wiki/Journalist "Journalist") [Nik Cohn](/wiki/Nik_Cohn "Nik Cohn") wrote a story called "[Tribal Rites of the New Saturday Night](/wiki/Tribal_Rites_of_the_New_Saturday_Night "Tribal Rites of the New Saturday Night")", about a young man in a [working-class](/wiki/Working-class "Working-class") [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn") neighborhood who, once a week, went to a local [disco](/wiki/Discotheque "Discotheque") called Odyssey 2001; the story was a sensation and served as the basis for the film *[Saturday Night Fever](/wiki/Saturday_Night_Fever "Saturday Night Fever")*. Twenty years later, in a followup story in *New York*,[[23]](#cite_note-23) Cohn admitted that he had made up the character and most of the story.

In 1976, the Australian media baron [Rupert Murdoch](/wiki/Rupert_Murdoch "Rupert Murdoch") bought the magazine in a [hostile takeover](/wiki/Hostile_takeover "Hostile takeover"), forcing Felker and Glaser out.[[24]](#cite_note-24) A succession of top editors followed through the remainder of the decade, including [James Brady](/wiki/James_Brady_(columnist) "James Brady (columnist)"), Joe Armstrong (who also served as publisher), [John Berendt](/wiki/John_Berendt "John Berendt"), and (briefly) [Jane Amsterdam](/wiki/Jane_Amsterdam "Jane Amsterdam").

### 1980s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(magazine)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1980s")]

In 1980, Murdoch hired [Edward Kosner](/wiki/Edward_Kosner "Edward Kosner"), the former editor of *[Newsweek](/wiki/Newsweek "Newsweek")*, to replace Armstrong. Murdoch also bought [*Cue*](/wiki/Cue_(magazine) "Cue (magazine)"), a [listings magazine](/wiki/Listings_magazine "Listings magazine") founded by Mort Glankoff that had covered the city since 1932, and folded it into *New York*, simultaneously creating a useful going-out guide and eliminating a competitor.[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-:0-26) Kosner's magazine shifted the mix of the magazine toward newsmagazine-style cover stories, trend pieces, and pure "service" features—long articles on shopping and other consumer subjects—as well as close coverage of the glitzy 1980s New York City scene epitomized by financiers [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") and [Saul Steinberg](/wiki/Saul_Steinberg_(business) "Saul Steinberg (business)"). The magazine was [profitable](/wiki/Profit_(economics) "Profit (economics)") for most of the 1980s.[[26]](#cite_note-:0-26) The term "the [Brat Pack](/wiki/Brat_Pack_(actors) "Brat Pack (actors)")" was coined for a 1985 cover story in the magazine.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

### 1990s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(magazine)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1990s")]

Murdoch got out of the magazine business in 1991 by selling his holdings to K-III Communications (now [Rent Group](/wiki/Rent_Group "Rent Group")), a partnership controlled by financier [Henry Kravis](/wiki/Henry_Kravis "Henry Kravis"). Subsequent budget pressure from K-III frustrated Kosner, and he left in 1993, taking over the editorship of [*Esquire*](/wiki/Esquire_(magazine) "Esquire (magazine)") magazine.[[28]](#cite_note-28) After several months during which the magazine was run by managing editor Peter Herbst, K-III hired [Kurt Andersen](/wiki/Kurt_Andersen "Kurt Andersen"), the co-creator of [*Spy*](/wiki/Spy_(magazine) "Spy (magazine)"), a humor monthly of the late 1980s and early 1990s. Andersen quickly replaced several staff members, bringing in emerging and established writers (including [Jim Cramer](/wiki/Jim_Cramer "Jim Cramer"), [Walter Kirn](/wiki/Walter_Kirn "Walter Kirn"), [Michael Tomasky](/wiki/Michael_Tomasky "Michael Tomasky"), and [Jacob Weisberg](/wiki/Jacob_Weisberg "Jacob Weisberg")) and editors (including Michael Hirschorn, Kim France, [Dany Levy](/w/index.php?title=Dany_Levy&action=edit&redlink=1 "Dany Levy (page does not exist)"), and Maer Roshan), and generally making the magazine faster-paced, younger in outlook, and more knowing in tone.[[29]](#cite_note-29) In August 1996, [Bill Reilly](/wiki/Bill_Reilly "Bill Reilly") fired Andersen from his editorship, citing the publication's financial results.[[30]](#cite_note-30) According to Andersen, he was fired for refusing to kill a story about a rivalry between investment bankers [Felix Rohatyn](/wiki/Felix_Rohatyn "Felix Rohatyn") and [Steven Rattner](/wiki/Steven_Rattner "Steven Rattner") that had upset [Henry Kravis](/wiki/Henry_Kravis "Henry Kravis"), a member of the firm's ownership group.[[31]](#cite_note-31) His replacement was Caroline Miller, who came from [*Seventeen*](/wiki/Seventeen_(American_magazine) "Seventeen (American magazine)"), another K-III title. In part owing to the company's financial constraints, Miller and her editors focused on cultivating younger writers, including [Ariel Levy](/wiki/Ariel_Levy_(writer) "Ariel Levy (writer)"), [Jennifer Senior](/wiki/Jennifer_Senior "Jennifer Senior"), [Robert Kolker](/wiki/Robert_Kolker "Robert Kolker"), and [Vanessa Grigoriadis](/wiki/Vanessa_Grigoriadis "Vanessa Grigoriadis").[[32]](#cite_note-32) She also hired [Michael Wolff](/wiki/Michael_Wolff_(journalist) "Michael Wolff (journalist)"), whose writing about media and politics became an extremely popular component of the magazine.

### 2000s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(magazine)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2000s")]

The magazine's first website, under the url nymetro.com, appeared in 2001.[[33]](#cite_note-33) In 2002 and 2003, [Wolff](/wiki/Michael_Wolff_(journalist) "Michael Wolff (journalist)"), the media critic Miller had hired in 1998, won two [National Magazine Awards](/wiki/National_Magazine_Awards "National Magazine Awards") for his columns. At the end of 2003, *New York* was sold again, to a family trust controlled by financier [Bruce Wasserstein](/wiki/Bruce_Wasserstein "Bruce Wasserstein"), for $55 million.[[34]](#cite_note-34) Wasserstein, early in 2004, replaced Miller with [Adam Moss](/wiki/Adam_Moss "Adam Moss"), who had founded the short-lived New York weekly *7 Days* and then edited *[The New York Times Magazine](/wiki/The_New_York_Times_Magazine "The New York Times Magazine")*.[[35]](#cite_note-35) That fall, Moss and his staff relaunched the magazine, most notably with two new sections: "The Strategist", devoted mostly to service, food, and shopping, and "The Culture Pages", covering the city's arts scene. Moss also rehired Kurt Andersen as a columnist. In early 2006, the company relaunched the magazine's website, previously nymetro.com, as nymag.com.

*New York* in this period won design awards at the [National Magazine Awards](/wiki/National_Magazine_Awards "National Magazine Awards") and was named Magazine of the Year by the [Society of Publication Designers](/w/index.php?title=Society_of_Publication_Designers&action=edit&redlink=1 "Society of Publication Designers (page does not exist)") (SPD) in 2006 and 2007. A 2008 cover about [Eliot Spitzer](/wiki/Eliot_Spitzer "Eliot Spitzer")'s prostitution scandal, created by the artist [Barbara Kruger](/wiki/Barbara_Kruger "Barbara Kruger") and displaying the word "Brain" with an arrow pointed at Spitzer's crotch, was named Cover of the Year by the [American Society of Magazine Editors](/wiki/American_Society_of_Magazine_Editors "American Society of Magazine Editors") (ASME) and *[Advertising Age](/wiki/Advertising_Age "Advertising Age")*. The next year, another cover, "[Bernie Madoff](/wiki/Bernie_Madoff "Bernie Madoff"), Monster", was named Best News & Business Cover by ASME. *New York* won back-to-back ASME Cover of the Year awards in 2012 and 2013, for "Is She Just Too Old for This?" and "The City and the Storm" respectively. Design director Chris Dixon and photography director Jody Quon were named "Design Team of the Year" by [Adweek](/wiki/Adweek "Adweek") in 2008.

When [Bruce Wasserstein](/wiki/Bruce_Wasserstein "Bruce Wasserstein") died in 2009, [David Carr](/wiki/David_Carr_(journalist) "David Carr (journalist)") of *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* wrote that "While previous owners had required constant features in the magazine about the best place to get a croissant or a beret, it was clear that Wasserstein wanted a publication that was the best place to learn about the complicated apparatus that is modern New York. In enabling as much, Mr. Wasserstein recaptured the original intent of the magazine's founder, [Clay Felker](/wiki/Clay_Felker "Clay Felker")."[[36]](#cite_note-36) Wasserstein's children retained control of the magazine, which continued to be overseen by his deputy Anup Bagaria.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

In 2006, *New York*'s website, NYMag.com, underwent a year-long relaunch, transforming from a site that principally republished the magazine's content to an up-to-the-minute news- and- service destination. In 2008, parent company New York Media also purchased the restaurant- and-menu site MenuPages as a complement to its own restaurant coverage, reselling it[[38]](#cite_note-38) in 2011 to Seamless.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

With the launch of Grub Street, devoted to food, and Daily Intelligencer (later renamed just "Intelligencer"), its politics site, both in 2006; Vulture, its culture site, in 2007; and The Cut, its fashion-and-women's-interest site, in 2008, *New York* began shifting significant resources toward digital-only publication. These sites were intended to adapt the urbane sensibility of the print magazine for a national and international audience, and attract readership that had been lost by print magazines in general, particularly fashion and entertainment outlets. By July 2010, digital ads accounted for one-third of the company's advertising revenue. [David Carr](/wiki/David_Carr_(journalist) "David Carr (journalist)") noted in an August 2010 column, "In a way, *New York* magazine is fast becoming a digital enterprise with a magazine attached."[[40]](#cite_note-40)

### 2010s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(magazine)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2010s")]

On March 1, 2011, it was announced that [Frank Rich](/wiki/Frank_Rich "Frank Rich") would leave *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* to become an essayist and editor-at-large for *New York*.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

*New York*'s "Encyclopedia of 9/11", published on the tenth anniversary of the attacks, was described by *[Gizmodo](/wiki/Gizmodo "Gizmodo")* as "heartbreaking, locked in the past, and entirely current"; the issue won a National Magazine Award for Single-Topic Issue.[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43)

In October 2012, *New York*'s offices in lower Manhattan were without electricity in the week following [Hurricane Sandy](/wiki/Hurricane_Sandy "Hurricane Sandy"), so the editorial staff published an issue from a quickly constructed temporary newsroom in the midtown office of Wasserstein & Company.[[44]](#cite_note-44) The issue's cover, shot by photographer Iwan Baan from a helicopter and showing Manhattan half in darkness, almost immediately became an iconic image of the storm;[[45]](#cite_note-45) *Time* called it the magazine cover of the year.[[46]](#cite_note-46) The image was republished as a poster by the Museum of Modern Art, with proceeds benefiting Hurricane Sandy relief efforts.[[47]](#cite_note-47) The following spring, *New York* took the top honor at the National Magazine Awards, again receiving the Magazine of the Year award for its print and digital coverage.[[5]](#cite_note-Haughney-5)

In December 2013, as readership for its digital sites continued to build, the magazine announced plans to shift the print edition to biweekly publication the following March, reducing from 42 issues per year to 26 plus three special editions.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

In April 2016, the magazine announced the launch of Select All, a new vertical dedicated to technology and innovation.[[49]](#cite_note-49) In 2019, Select All was shuttered and folded into the broadened "Intelligencer" news site.

In the mid-2010s, *New York* launched several podcasts jointly produced with other outlets, all short-lived. Its first independently owned podcast, Good One: A Podcast About Jokes, hosted by Jesse David Fox, launched in February 2017. The magazine also expanded into television, collaborating with Michael Hirschorn's Ish Entertainment and [Bravo](/wiki/Bravo_(US_TV_channel) "Bravo (US TV channel)") to produce a pilot for a weekly. TV show based on its popular back-page feature, the Approval Matrix.[[50]](#cite_note-50) *New York*'s art critic Jerry Saltz appeared as a judge on Bravo's reality competition series *[Work of Art: The Next Great Artist](/wiki/Work_of_Art:_The_Next_Great_Artist "Work of Art: The Next Great Artist")* in 2010 and 2011.[[51]](#cite_note-51) Grub Street senior editor Alan Sytsma appeared as a guest on judge on three episodes of the [third season of *Top Chef Masters*](/wiki/Top_Chef_Masters_(season_3) "Top Chef Masters (season 3)").

April 2018 was *New York*'s 50th anniversary, marked with a book-length history of the magazine and its city, published by [Simon & Schuster](/wiki/Simon_%26_Schuster "Simon & Schuster") and titled *Highbrow, Lowbrow, Brilliant, Despicable: 50 Years of New York*. The magazine also produced a commemorative issue and celebrated with a party at [Katz's Delicatessen](/wiki/Katz%27s_Delicatessen "Katz's Delicatessen"). That year, The Cut introduced its podcast, "The Cut on Tuesdays", produced jointly with Gimlet Media and hosted by one of the site's writers, Molly Fischer.

In December 2018, *New York*'s fashion and beauty destination site, The Cut, carried a piece titled "Is [Priyanka Chopra](/wiki/Priyanka_Chopra "Priyanka Chopra") and [Nick Jonas](/wiki/Nick_Jonas "Nick Jonas")'s Love for Real?", that drew severe backlash from readers for accusing [Chopra](/wiki/Priyanka_Chopra "Priyanka Chopra") of trapping [Jonas](/wiki/Nick_Jonas "Nick Jonas") into a fraudulent relationship and calling her a "global scam artist". The publication removed the piece the following morning and issued an apology.[[52]](#cite_note-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53)

In January 2019, Moss announced that he was retiring from the editorship. [David Haskell](/wiki/David_Haskell_(editor) "David Haskell (editor)"), one of his chief deputies, succeeded him as editor on April 1, 2019. That spring, the magazine laid off several staff members and temporary employees.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

In his first few months in the role, Haskell published a cover story on a bizarre cult at Sarah Lawrence college that ultimately led to its leader Larry Ray's conviction on federal charges of extortion, sex trafficking, and racketeering conspiracy.[[55]](#cite_note-55) The magazine also published an excerpt from E. Jean Carroll's book that accused then-president Donald Trump of sexual assault and would have ongoing legal ramifications for him.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

On September 24, 2019, [Vox Media](/wiki/Vox_Media "Vox Media") announced that it had purchased the magazine's parent company, New York Media LLC.[[9]](#cite_note-Tracy-9) Pam Wasserstein, the CEO of New York Media, became Vox Media's president, working closely with its CEO, [Jim Bankoff](/wiki/Jim_Bankoff "Jim Bankoff").

### 2020s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(magazine)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2020s")]

After the merger with Vox Media, May 2020, [Vox Media](/wiki/Vox_Media "Vox Media") announced it was merging the real estate site *[Curbed](/wiki/Curbed "Curbed")* into *New York* and refocusing the site on its roots in New York City.[[57]](#cite_note-57) That year, *New York* also expanded its podcast business,[[58]](#cite_note-58) adding *Pivot*, *On With Kara Swisher*, *Where Should We Begin with Esther Perel*, *[Switched on Pop](/wiki/Switched_on_Pop "Switched on Pop")*, and *Into It With Sam Sanders* to its lineup. The company also saw an expansion of its intellectual property into television and movies, notably with *[Hustlers](/wiki/Hustlers_(film) "Hustlers (film)")*, a feature film adapted from a story by Jessica Pressler. In 2022, three television series adapted from *New York* properties appeared: *Inventing Anna* and *The Watcher* on Netflix, and *Sex Diaries* on HBO. The magazine also moved into publishing an array of digital newsletters, including "Are U Coming?", which documented the nightlife of city emerging from Covid lockdown; "The Year I Ate New York", written in 2022 by Tammie Taclamarian and in 2023 by E. Alex Jung; and a collection of limited-series newsletters devoted to [*Succession*](/wiki/Succession_(TV_series) "Succession (TV series)"), [*…And Just Like That*](/wiki/And_Just_Like_That... "And Just Like That..."), and prominent New York City court cases.

Notable stories published by *New York* in this decade include [Nicholson Baker](/wiki/Nicholson_Baker "Nicholson Baker")'s investigation of the possibility that a lab leak instigated the COVID-19 epidemic; a cover package, "Ten Years Since Trayvon," about the rise of the [Black Lives Matter](/wiki/Black_Lives_Matter "Black Lives Matter") movement; "The Year of the [Nepo Baby](/wiki/Nepo_baby "Nepo baby")," a widely discussed feature about dynastic career advancement in Hollywood, that popularized the term and won a National Magazine Award; and “There Is No Safe Word,” an in-depth look at sexual-assault allegations against [Neil Gaiman](/wiki/Neil_Gaiman "Neil Gaiman"), after which he was dropped by his publisher.[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60) [Lindsay Peoples](/wiki/Lindsay_Peoples_Wagner "Lindsay Peoples Wagner") became the editor of The Cut in 2021, and *New York* hired book critic [Andrea Long Chu](/wiki/Andrea_Long_Chu "Andrea Long Chu"), who subsequently won the Pulitzer Prize for Criticism.

## Puzzles and competitions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(magazine)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Puzzles and competitions")]

*New York* magazine has long run literary competitions and distinctive [crossword puzzles](/wiki/Crossword_puzzles "Crossword puzzles"). For the first year of the magazine's existence, the composer and lyricist [Stephen Sondheim](/wiki/Stephen_Sondheim "Stephen Sondheim") contributed an extremely complex [cryptic crossword](/wiki/Cryptic_crossword "Cryptic crossword") to every third issue. Sondheim eventually ceded the job in order to write his next musical, and [Richard Maltby, Jr.](/wiki/Richard_Maltby,_Jr. "Richard Maltby, Jr.") took over. For many years the magazine also syndicated [The Times](/wiki/The_Times "The Times") of London's cryptic crossword.

Beginning in early 1969, for two weeks out of every three, Sondheim's friend Mary Ann Madden edited[[61]](#cite_note-61) an extremely popular witty literary competition calling for readers to send in humorous poetry or other bits of wordplay on a given theme that changed with each installment. (A typical entry, in a competition calling for humorous epitaphs, supplied this one for Geronimo: "Requiescat in Apache.") Altogether, Madden ran 973 installments of the competition, retiring in 2000. Hundreds, sometimes thousands, of entries were received each week, and winners included [David Mamet](/wiki/David_Mamet "David Mamet"), [Herb Sargent](/wiki/Herb_Sargent "Herb Sargent"), and [Dan Greenburg](/wiki/Dan_Greenburg "Dan Greenburg"). [David Halberstam](/wiki/David_Halberstam "David Halberstam") once claimed that he had submitted entries 137 times without winning. Madden published three volumes of Competition winners, titled *Thank You for the Giant Sea Tortoise*, *Son of Giant Sea Tortoise*, and *Maybe He's Dead: And Other Hilarious Results of New York Magazine Competitions*.

Beginning in 1980, the magazine ran an American-style crossword constructed by [Maura B. Jacobson](/wiki/Maura_Jacobson "Maura Jacobson"). Jacobson retired in April 2011, having created 1,400 puzzles for the magazine, after which the job passed to Cathy Allis Millhauser and then Matt Gaffney.[[62]](#cite_note-62) In January 2020, [*Vulture*](/wiki/Vulture_(website) "Vulture (website)") began publishing daily 10x10 crosswords by two constructors, Malaika Handa and Stella Zawistowski.[[63]](#cite_note-63) Vulture continued its expansion into games with the launch of Cinematrix, an addictive movie trivia game, in 2024, followed by Telematrix in 2025.[[64]](#cite_note-64)[[65]](#cite_note-65)

## Sites operated by *New York*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(magazine)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Sites operated by New York")]

### *Intelligencer*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(magazine)&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Intelligencer")]

*New York*'s news blog was introduced under the name *Daily Intelligencer*, expanding upon the weekly magazine's front-of-the-book Intelligencer section. Launched in 2006, it was initially written mostly by [Jessica Pressler](/wiki/Jessica_Pressler "Jessica Pressler") and Chris Rovzar, whose coverage focused on local politics, media, and Wall Street but also included extensive chatter about the television show *Gossip Girl*. Over its first half-decade, the site expanded in reach and became more focused on national politics, notably with the addition of columnist [Jonathan Chait](/wiki/Jonathan_Chait "Jonathan Chait") in 2011 and the longtime political blogger Ed Kilgore in 2015.

### *The Cut*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(magazine)&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: The Cut")]

Main article: [The Cut (publication)](/wiki/The_Cut_(publication) "The Cut (publication)")

*The Cut* launched on the *New York* website in 2008, edited by Amy Odell, to replace a previous [fashion week](/wiki/Fashion_week "Fashion week") blog, *Show & Talk*.[[66]](#cite_note-Lieber-66) In 2012 it became a standalone website,[[67]](#cite_note-67) shifting focus from fashion to women's issues more generally.[[66]](#cite_note-Lieber-66) Stella Bugbee became editor-in-chief in 2017, and presided over a relaunch that appeared on August 21.[[68]](#cite_note-68) The new site was designed for an enhanced mobile-first experience and to better reflect the topics covered.[[69]](#cite_note-69) In January 2018, *The Cut* published Moira Donegan's essay revealing her as the creator of the controversial "[Shitty Media Men](/wiki/Shitty_Media_Men "Shitty Media Men")" list, a viral but short-lived anonymous spreadsheet [crowdsourcing](/wiki/Crowdsourcing "Crowdsourcing") unconfirmed reports of sexual misconduct by men in journalism.[[70]](#cite_note-70) That August, the site also published "Everywhere and Nowhere," Lindsay Peoples's essay about the fashion industry's inhospitability to Black voices and points of view.[[71]](#cite_note-71) In 2019, *The Cut* published an excerpt from [E. Jean Carroll](/wiki/E._Jean_Carroll "E. Jean Carroll")'s book, *What Do We Need Men For? A Modest Proposal,* mostly about [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump")'s sexual assault on her.[[72]](#cite_note-72) In 2021, Peoples became the site's next editor-in-chief. *The Cut* also incorporates the pop-science rubric *Science of Us*, which previously existed as a standalone site. In 2024, 'The Cut' published an article in which a woman confessed to abusing and neglecting her pet cat, Lucky. Advocates took to social media by storm seeking justice for Lucky.

### *Grub Street*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(magazine)&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Grub Street")]

*Grub Street*, covering food and restaurants, was expanded in 2009 to five additional cities served by former nymag.com sister site MenuPages.com.[[73]](#cite_note-73) In 2013 *Grub Street* announced that it would close its city blogs outside New York and bring a more national focus to GrubStreet.com.[[74]](#cite_note-74)

### *Vulture*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(magazine)&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Vulture")]

Main article: [Vulture (website)](/wiki/Vulture_(website) "Vulture (website)")

*Vulture* was launched as a pop culture blog on NYMag.com in 2007. It moved to an independent web address, Vulture.com, in 2012. In 2018, New York Media acquired the comedy news blog *[Splitsider](/wiki/The_Awl "The Awl")*, folding the operation into the *Vulture* website.[[75]](#cite_note-75)

### *The Strategist*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(magazine)&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: The Strategist")]

In 2016, *New York* launched the *Strategist*, an expansion of a column from the print version of *New York* Magazine that aimed to help readers navigate shopping from the *New York* perspective. The site joined other [product review sites](/wiki/Review_site "Review site") focusing on providing free product reviews to readers, generating affiliate commissions when readers would purchase a product they recommended. The early editorial team included editors David Haskell and Alexis Swerdloff. Popular recurring franchises include the celebrity-shopping "What I Can't Live Without" series, "Strategist-Approved" gift guides, and beauty reviews by influencer Rio Viera-Newton. The *Strategist* does not publish branded content that is paid for by the subject of a story, but it earns revenue through [affiliate advertising](/wiki/Affiliate_marketing "Affiliate marketing"), including the Amazon Associates Program. In 2018, the *Strategist* experimented with a holiday pop-up shop called I Found It at the Strategist.[[76]](#cite_note-76)

### *Curbed*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_(magazine)&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Curbed")]

In 2020, *New York* took over the Vox Media website *[Curbed](/wiki/Curbed "Curbed")*, which had begun by covering New York City real estate and development since 2005 and had grown to cover urbanism and design news in many American cities. That October, Curbed relaunched as a *New York* vertical with a new design and a resharpened focus on New York City. Its prominent writers include the Pulitzer Prize–winner [Justin Davidson](/wiki/Justin_Davidson "Justin Davidson"), the magazine's architecture critic, and Wendy Goodman, its design editor.
